data.title: High Yield Smackdown
data.description:
Remember a few weeks ago when I was in all “Chicken Little” mode?

Sure, let’s call it that. Seems a fair characterization for warning investors not to pay utterly absurd price-to-book ratios.

For instance, I wrote:

“I keep running my mouth about the price-to-book ratio. Some people (awful investors) think price-to-book doesn’t matter. Those investors thoroughly disagree with the executives of the REITs, so that’s pretty funny. There are times when price-to-book can be overshadowed by other factors. For instance, if a REIT was fatally flawed. That’s pretty rare, but it can happen.”

I also wrote:

“My preferred choice, sorry bad pun, is to play it safe. I typically lean more toward the preferred shares. When I venture into the common shares, usually it's for a short-term trade. I’m not interested in jumping into the common shares to see if management can deliver some outstanding performance with their portfolio. Yuck! When I jump into common shares, I want to see the price-to-book ratio do something that's extremely common in most fields around the world: "regression to the mean." That’s it. I just want to see the price-to-book ratio normalize. When I can get a big discount to the normal valuations, then that’s probably attractive. Otherwise, I can sit in some of the less volatile securities.

Preferred shares are often one of my favorite areas. I can collect a respectable yield while waiting for relative values to swing or for big opportunities to show up in the common shares. A few preferred shares are still trading below my targets, but not very many. It’s a significant contrast to a few years ago. We were spoiled for choice for a while. Lately, I’ve been much more cautious in our portfolio strategy.”

REITs Got Wrecked

I was really hammering on the agency mortgage REITs for their high price-to-book ratios. How did they perform since then?

Note: This chart ends with the market close on 4/8/2025. So it could be wildly off in the future. I don’t have a solution for that. That’s just how screenshots and time work.

Update: I was planning to push this out the night of 4/8/2025, but there were some delays. On the morning of 4/9/2025, mortgage REITs got dumped even further. Quite a bit further. Then in the span of about 20 minutes, they had a huge rally (with the rest of the market) when tariffs, except for tariffs on China, were “paused” for 90 days. Of course, that pause could be unpaused (because that's how pauses work), so who actually knows? I would have to be editing this article and reposting it multiple times per day. Instead, I'll try to just post a new one occasionally to assist readers.

Returning to the Article

The S&P 500 got slapped around also. Down about 12% for the same period. Still significant, but less of a train wreck. Would you believe there are actually bargains in the batch now? We’re not permanently bearish on The REIT Forum. Scott Kennedy even picked up shares in one of the agency mortgage REITs (among a few purchases). I may do the same soon as well. Hard to say. That was the plan, then prices went lower, then higher, so I can't really commit to any future trades.

Note: Scott picked up those shares. I have not yet, so you won't find an agency mortgage REIT in my disclosure list.

Why So Busy?

Sorry, I haven’t sent much research to the public side. You may be familiar with this whole tariff thing. It’s making the market exciting again, but it’s also pretty hard to predict. We went from a 20% tariff on China prior to Liberation Day to a 104% tariff set to take effect as soon as we ring in 4/9/2025. I would say "tomorrow," but some people will be reading this on different days. We’ve got updates on a ton of stocks. But you know what’s hard to do for updates? Industrial REITs. Do you know why? Because tariffs of 20%, 54%, and 104% are materially different.

So this will be a very short article. It’s a chance to post the charts for the sector. The charts are using book value as of 12/31/2025. We have estimates for 3/31/2025 and more estimates for 4/4/2025, but I think you can understand why those aren’t going into the article. Those figures are all in our service and the latest figures are in the Google Sheets.

Modeling

If you know how to model for tariffs going from 20% to 104%, I would love to hear about it. We’ve never had tariffs remotely close to these levels. Not even the ones that prolonged the Great Depression. That sure was a great time. Since such tariffs have never existed before, I’ll have to assume anyone who can model the impacts effectively is joining us in the future. Perhaps to stop the tariffs, or perhaps to laugh at how everything unfolded. If that’s you, I have some questions. I’ll just ask you to demonstrate you’re from the future by sending me a copy of the first article I publish in June 2025.

Industrial REITs

This is easily the most challenging part of massive tariffs. The impact on the other sectors can be significant, but industrial REITs face even greater challenges. Imagine being their tenant and finding out that in a few months your tariffs went from 0% to 20%, and then in a week, they're going from 20% to 104%. If you believe the company sending the imports from China is going to pay the taxes, you’re going to be really surprised about how taxes work. Of course, I’ll probably add to my position in industrial REITs. Prices got hammered. It’s still possible that much of the decline could be erased with a tweet.

All The Stocks

The charts compare the following companies and their preferred shares or baby bonds:

  • BDCs: (CSWC), (BXSL), (TSLX), (OCSL), (GAIN), (TPVG), (FSK), (MAIN), (ARCC), (GBDC), (OBDC), (SLRC).
  • Commercial mREITs: (GPMT), (FBRT), (BXMT).
  • Residential Hybrid mREITs: (MITT), (CIM), (RC), (MFA), (EFC), (NYMT).
  • Residential Agency mREITs: (NLY), (AGNC), (CHMI), (DX), (TWO), (ARR), (ORC).
  • Residential Originator and Servicer mREITs: (RITM), (PMT).

Embedded Charts

Mortgage REITs and BDCs:

Preferred shares and baby bonds:

Thanks for reading and I hope you enjoyed the charts.

Some terminology:

  • FTF = Fixed-to-floating. The share is currently fixed but will begin floating based on SOFR. We may reference LIBOR, but that's assumed to be SOFR + 0.26161%.
  • FTR = Fixed-to-reset. The share is currently fixed. It will eventually begin resetting every five years based on the five-year Treasury rate.
  • FTL = Fixed-to-lawsuit. The company decided that their FTF shares could be "fixed-to-fixed" despite clearly violating the original intent of the contract.
  • Floating = A share that was FTF, but is now floating. The dividend rate is updated every three months.

Thank You

Build a foundation for steady income with REITs

As demand for key real estate sectors increases and supply fails to keep pace, 2025 presents a prime opportunity to invest in REITs, Preferreds, and BDCs.

The REIT Forum offers a proven, transparent approach with actionable insights. Led by Colorado Wealth Management Fund (aka Michael VanLoon) and an expert team, subscribers receive precise trade alerts, portfolio tracking, and exclusive articles. Strategies from The REIT Forum have consistently outperformed sector indexes for nearly a decade.

Start your risk-free two-week trial today and capitalize on the power of real estate returns.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EUR/USD Outlook: Euro Benefits From U.S.-China Escalation, Markets Price In April ECB Rate Cut
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

EUR/USD has been on a good run this week and jumped this morning as tensions between the US and China escalated. The latest escalation has left the US Dollar, and in particular the US Dollar index (DXY), on the back foot.

The DXY is testing last week's lows at the time of writing, and this has helped EUR/USD rise despite markets pricing an ECB rate cut in April.

US Dollar Index Daily Chart, April 9, 2025

The performance of the Euro has surprised me to say the least. This morning, we saw traders fully price ECB rate cuts in April for the first time. This was followed by Morgan Stanley lowering the Euro area's 2025 GDP forecast to 0.8% vs the prior forecast of 1.0% while also saying that they expect the ECB benchmark rate to reach 1.5% in December 2025 vs the prior forecast of June 2026.

This was further echoed by a Reuters report, which stated that the European Central Bank (ECB) now expects Trump’s tariffs to hurt the Eurozone's growth more than first thought. Sources say the earlier estimate of a 0.5 percentage point impact is too low, with one suggesting it could exceed 1 percentage point.

These are supposedly dovish moves for the Euro, which under normal circumstances might have seen a market reaction. However, given the dynamics around the trade war, markets have been focusing elsewhere. We have discussed this at length of late, the trade war overshadowing economic data releases and news. This trend seems to be intact for now.

US-China trade war escalates

US President Donald Trump's additional tariffs on China went into effect yesterday, with China refusing to bow to President Trump's wishes. Instead, Chinese authorities through the Finance Ministry announced that it will impose additional tariffs of 84% on US goods to come into effect on April 10.

The news sent risk assets like the S&P 500 tumbling this morning as fears ramped up over further escalation. China added more firm to the unreliable list as China's Premier Li chaired a symposium on the economic situation with experts and businesses.

Premier Li acknowledged that while external factors may cause pressure, the government is ready to deal with it. Premier Li also touched on expanding local demand, something he called a long-term goal.

The Federal Reserve (Fed) will release the minutes from its March meeting later today. However, since this meeting happened before the tariff announcements, traders might not pay much attention to it as tariffs continue to dominate the discourse.

If this is the case, expect any further tit-for-tat between US-China to lead to potential EUR strength against the US dollar based on recent history.

Technical Analysis on EUR/USD

EUR/USD Daily Chart, April 9, 2025

Looking at the EUR/USD daily chart, the pair is on course for a third successive day of gains.

The pair is currently testing an area of resistance at the 1.1100 hurdle, can the rally continue?

There is definitely scope for further upside, but I think that will require a prolonged standoff between the US-China. Any deal that may arise between the two could send EUR/USD tumbling.

As we have seen above, tariffs are having an impact on everything from growth to monetary policy at present.

Looking at the period 14-RSI, and it is approaching overbought levels once more. This means that a retracement the likes of which occurred on April 3 could repeat itself.

This is definitely worth monitoring moving forward.

Key levels to pay attention to

Support

  • 1.1000
  • 1.0948
  • 1.0900

Resistance

  • 1.1100
  • 1.1200
  • 1.1250

Original Post

data.title: JetBlue: Harmful Downturn (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
As with the industry, JetBlue Airways Corporation (NASDAQ:JBLU) is under pressure with earnings expectations slipping. The airline faces additional pressure, having failed to turn the corner back towards generating profits and positive cash flows. My investment thesis is more Neutral on JetBlue Airways, with a preference to sit on the sidelines waiting for a clearer picture and better market signals.

Solid Delta Look

Delta Air Lines (DAL) provided an early look at the airline sector's expectations for Q2 and the rest of the year. The weak stock market and ongoing global trade war were clearly expected to impact travel going forward, but Delta provided comfort to the market with updated guidance generally better than expected.

The legacy airline guided to Q2 earnings in the range of $2.00 versus consensus analyst estimates of $2.29 with plans to repay debt of up to $3 billion this year. The numbers were solid and again provided a sign of how the airline sector is different now, with the ability to cut costs and lower capacity quickly to offset demand shocks like the trade war.

Unfortunately, though, JetBlue faces bigger problems than Delta. The airline is struggling to return to producing profits and any reduction to demand will hit the business much harder, whether the numbers are cut very much or not.

Back in March, JetBlue followed the initial warning by Delta Air Lines to cut Q1 estimates. The company provided the following guidance update on March 11.

In essence, JetBlue lowered some of the expectations for revenue while keeping CASM ex-fuel costs up at 8% to 10% growth. On the flip side, the airline should see lower fuel costs, especially flowing into Q2 considering the updated guidance and impacted oil prices were late in the quarter.

Either way, analysts forecast the airline reporting a massive $0.62 loss in Q1, even worse than last year. JetBlue reinforced the JetForward plans when reporting yearly results in January, but the airline just hasn't turned the corner on better results yet, and the global trade war isn't going to help.

The airline has plans to generate $800 to $900 million in incremental EBIT by 2027, but the process is only getting started. JetBlue needs to show these improvements much faster, with the $200 million targeted improvement in 2025 not actually leading to higher EPS estimates for this year.

Slipping Estimates

The consensus analyst estimates have JetBlue reporting a $1.09 EPS loss this year. The airline has approximately 350 million shares outstanding, so a $200 million boost to EBIT in 2025 should've helped wipe out a lot of the losses from last year, but the current forecasted losses are already larger than 2024, and slipping fast.

In essence, JetBlue is only preventing the losses from getting worse by implementing plans to improve the network and increase perks for loyal and premium customers. The airline faces accelerated risk, with 2+ years of losses expected into what Jamie Dimon of JPMorgan Chase (JPM) just forecasted as a recession ahead.

JetBlue is a prime example of what happens to a corporation that doesn't become financially strong during a booming market. Airline traffic hit record levels during 2024 and the JetBlue still reported large losses.

If the trade wars subside and JetBlue still has this path to boost EBIT by up to $900 million through 2027, the stock can become an aggressive pick again. JetBlue needs the clear runway to survive through the ultimate goal to ensure investors reach the full benefits from the JetForward program.

The airline ended 2024 with a cash balance of $3.9 billion, along with total debt of $8.5 billion. JetBlue wouldn't appear to face any dire outcomes considering the very large cash balance, but the company needs to show the ability to progress towards generating profits, even during downturns.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Delta Air Lines provided some solid signs the major airlines will survive the current trade wars with solid profits and cash flows, though lower than originally expected. Unfortunately, JetBlue faces far bigger issues having failed to fully turnaround the business during the boom times, leading to rather large loss expectations for the year.

Investors should watch JetBlue Airways from the sidelines until the airline has a much clearly path to turning the large ongoing losses into a profitable future.

data.title: ON Semiconductor Is No Bargain (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

After declining over 50% in the past six months, ON Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:ON) stock is still no bargain. In the below analysis, the technicals show that there is more pain to come as both classical and Ichimoku Cloud analyses show that weakness is likely to continue for the stock. As for the fundamentals, most recent earnings were concerning as there are widespread declines in their financials. To make matters worse, Q1 guidance shows that the deterioration is expected to accelerate. The P/S ratio's major contraction reflects this accurately, making the stock more or less fairly valued even though it's at a discount to the IT sector. Overall, I believe ON Semiconductor is a sell as the technicals show that the declines have no end in sight while the fundamentals are unconvincing.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

ON Semiconductor has been in a downtrend since late November. On the current trading day, the stock has fallen beneath the lower channel line, perhaps signalling an escalation in the rate of declines. There is now heavy resistance over the stock versus no support in this timeframe as there are three key levels above in addition to the channel lines. The nearest level is at around 41.5. This area was support in early to mid-March, while being a bit of resistance during the bounce at the start of this month. The next resistance level is at around 47 as this area represents support in early February and some resistance in early March. Lastly, there is relatively distant resistance at around 58, which represents a downside gap in January that occurred with the highest bearish volume in the past six months.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA has remained below the 200-day SMA for the past six months, and the gap between the lines is continuing to expand. This shows that long term bearish momentum is accelerating. As for the EMAs, there hasn't been a crossover in months as well, with the 9-day EMA being consistently below the 21-day EMA. This demonstrates sustained bearishness in the stock. After a brief period of the gap between the EMAs contracting in late March, it is now expanding again as the stock has resumed its major declines.

The Bollinger Band's midline most recently acted as resistance for the stock in late March, signalling that the downtrend remains highly intact. Currently, ON Semiconductor sits below the lower band and so it is likely oversold. The MACD had a bearish crossover very early this month as a near term bearish signal and the histogram has been bearish since late March. However, as shown above, the MACD hasn't made a major new six months low despite the plunge in stock and so the bears may be weaker than previously thought. Unlike the MACD, the RSI has made fresh six months lows during the latest declines, confirming the bearishness in the stock. Currently, the RSI is at around 23, well, beneath 30 and shows that the bears have a stranglehold as of now. Lastly, for the stochastics, the %K broke beneath the 50 level in late March and also had a bearish crossover with the %D. Now, both lines sit within the 20 zone, another indication that the bears have overwhelming control of the stock.

Takeaway

In my view, these classic technical analysis tools clearly show a bearish outlook for ON Semiconductor. While it's no surprise that the technicals are currently weak due to the global markets' selloff, the stock has been weak for a long period even before the rout. The chart indicates that the downtrend is accelerating, the moving averages show increasing bearish momentum in both the near and longer terms, and the indicators have plenty of bearish signs. The only positive was that the MACD hasn't made a significant new low yet, which means that perhaps hope is not completely lost for the bulls.

Fibonacci

Time Zones

At the 13th day mark, the stock was able to begin a minor rally while on the 21st day mark, there was a major bearish candle. The Fibonacci Time Zones seem to have a notable effect on the stock and may continue to do so moving forward. The next significant day for ON Semiconductor is the 34th day, which will be on April 16th. Investors should expect significant price movement and/or volatility around that date due to the stock's past interaction with the Fibonacci Time Zones.

Projection

For the near term, the February peak was a significant one, and so that is used for this projection. The early to mid-March trough and the retracement to around the 45 level are also used as the inputs here. On the current trading day, ON Semiconductor has broken beneath the 61.8% level, which was just above 35. Now, the next level of support is likely to be at the 100% level, which is a hair below 30. With this Fibonacci Projection level so near a major round number, support there is likely to be substantial and investors can expect the stock to bounce at that level if further declines occur.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

The stock is currently far below the cloud, indicating strong bearishness overall. In the past six months or so, ON Semiconductor has spent the vast majority of the time below the cloud, and so clearly bearishness has been sustained. The cloud itself has also been highly bearish with the Leading Span A being consistently below the Leading Span B. The cloud is significantly thick moving to the end of April but does show some thinning in May and so resistance may start to weaken a bit by then. However, the stock may continue to get pressured as the cloud moves downward. The Conversion line and the baseline had an overlap period in late February to early March to show indecision, but the Conversion line ultimately dropped back below, showing that bearish momentum remains dominant in the near term. Lastly, the Lagging Span is far below mid-February levels in the stock, confirming that the downtrend is strong at this point.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

ON Semiconductor last reported earnings in February for their 2024 Q4 and showed some worrying results. The company posted revenues of $1.7 billion, down from $2.0 billion in the prior year period. For EPS, they had an adjusted figure of $0.95, a 24% decline YoY. Both figures also underperformed consensus expectations as revenue missed by $35.83 million while EPS missed by $0.02. As shown above, revenue growth is still far below average levels, despite showing some QoQ improvement over their Q3. As for cash from operations, they reported $579.7 million, which still represents a modest decline from the year ago quarter. As you can see above, growth for this figure is still at poor levels relative to the past few years. Lastly, both gross and operating margins showed some potentially worrying signs. Adjusted gross margin declined from 46.7% to 45.3% while operating margin declined from 31.6% to 26.7%, showing that are concerning trends with the efficiency of the business. Overall, despite some moderation in the decline of the financials, I believe these earnings do not provide investors with the confidence they seek.

Guidance

As part of their earnings report, the company provided guidance for their 2025 Q1. First, they expect revenues to be between $1.35 billion and $1.45 billion. At the midpoint, this guidance would imply YoY declines of 24.8% in revenue versus the year ago quarter's $1.86 billion. This is an acceleration in the declines in revenue, as Q4 only saw 14.65% YoY declines. For EPS, they are projecting an adjusted figure between $0.45 and $0.55. With 2024 Q1 EPS being $1.08, the midpoint represents decline of 53.7% YoY, also a major acceleration from Q4's 24% decline. Lastly, adjusted gross margin is expected to range from 39% to 41%, a significant worsening from the prior year period's 45.9%. As a whole, there's not much to look forward to for ON Semiconductor, as clearly this outlook shows that the business is expected to weaken from an already fragile state.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has dropped to three-year lows and is therefore far below average. Currently, it has a reading of 2.133 after starting 2025 near the 4.0 level. I believe this contraction in the multiple is justified, as the stock is fairly valued at current levels. As discussed above, revenue growth remains at far below averages levels at -14.65% even though there was a minor QoQ improvement from their Q3. Furthermore, their guidance for Q1 shows that the declines in revenues are expected to accelerate sharply, as they project -25% growth. This would set a new three-year low for revenue growth and so therefore, the P/S ratio's contraction to multi-year lows accurately reflects the deterioration of the financials. Currently, the Information Technology sector has a median P/S ratio of 2.43 and so ON Semiconductor is at a 12.2% discount to peers. Given their highly concerning growth forecast, I wouldn't be surprised if this discount relative to the sector continued to increase from here.

Conclusion

50% off usually sounds like a great deal, but with six-month declines totalling 52%, ON Semiconductor is still no bargain. The technicals were shown to be very bearish, with both classical technical analysis and Ichimoku Clouds showing that the downtrend remains strong as bearish momentum continues to dominate. For the fundamentals, as discussed above, Q4 earnings were concerning but Q1 guidance was even worse as the financials show no signs of a turnaround. The P/S ratio does reflect this accurately, however, as the multiple's contraction to multiyear lows makes the stock fairly valued at current levels. With the stock still not being cheap and the technicals being highly bearish, I believe ON Semiconductor is a solid sell.

data.title: Don't Expect Non-GAAP Earnings To Exclude Tariffs - That's A Good Thing
data.description:
As investors contend with the difficult task of assessing the impact of tariffs and their consequences, one question that’s emerged is: will companies adjust their non-GAAP earnings measures to exclude tariffs? A corollary: tariffs are taxes, so are they added back in earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA)?

Since companies generally did not make non-GAAP adjustments for prior tariffs (e.g., tariffs imposed by the United States on Chinese imports in 2018), and based on our understanding of the Securities and Exchange Commission’s guidance on non-GAAP financial measures, we think the answer is no. But we do expect companies to quantify and call out the impact of tariffs, if they are material, leaving it up to investors to ignore or incorporate the impact of tariffs in valuation.

Tariffs are recurring costs that are settled in cash. Tariffs may also be passed on to customers. We think “adjusting out” the impact of tariffs is inappropriate, akin to adjusting out rising or falling commodity prices. Call outs by managers to quantify the impact are very helpful, however, as investors parse out the drivers of performance from one period to the next.

Very few companies adjusted earnings for tariffs in recent years

We searched the population of non-GAAP reconciliations of SEC filers for “tariff” using Calcbench. We found mentions by 22 companies, but fewer than five instances where the impact of tariffs was excluded from a non-GAAP financial measure.

This is a tiny subset of the >175 of S&P 500 companies that mentioned “tariffs” in their earnings calls in 2018 and >250 companies that did so in 2024, according to FactSet Earnings Insight. (We don’t have data for mentions of “tariff” among all SEC filers that host earnings calls, but those numbers are very likely higher).

One of the rare examples is in Harley-Davidson’s Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2019 earnings release furnished on Form 8-K with the SEC, an excerpt of which is reproduced below.

“The non-GAAP measures included in this press release are adjusted net income and adjusted diluted EPS excluding restructuring plan costs and the impact of recent EU and China tariffs. Restructuring plan costs include restructuring expenses as presented in the consolidated statements of income and costs associated with temporary inefficiencies incurred in connection with the manufacturing optimization initiative included in Motorcycles and Related Products cost of goods sold. The impact of recent EU and China tariffs includes incremental European Union and China tariffs imposed beginning in 2018 on the company’s products shipped from the U.S., as well as incremental U.S. tariffs imposed beginning in 2018 on certain items imported from China. The impact of recent EU and China tariffs excludes higher metals cost resulting from the U.S. steel and aluminum tariffs. These adjustments are consistent with the approach used for 2018 to determine performance relative to financial objectives under the company’s incentive compensation plans. A reconciliation of these non-GAAP measures from the comparable GAAP measure is included later in this press release.”Source: Harley-Davidson Announces Fourth Quarter, Full-Year 2019 Results

Harley-Davidson (HOG) discontinued the tariff-related non-GAAP adjustments after the European Union suspended tariffs on the company’s products in late 2021, though the tariffs on items imported from China remain in place.

While quantifying the impact of the tariffs is helpful, the overall usefulness of this non-GAAP adjustment is limited by the fact that it may not be possible to quantify earnings as if tariffs were never imposed. Tariffs interact with the business environment in numerous and complex ways. Absent from Harley-Davidson’s disclosure above, for example, is whether the cost of the tariffs was passed through to customers, whether it affected competitors and substitute products to the same degree, and whether the company took any offsetting cost actions.

Our understanding of the SEC’s guidance is that adjusting out tariffs is inappropriate

Besides scant historical evidence, another reason we believe companies will not adjust out tariffs in their non-GAAP earnings is the SEC staff’s Compliance & Disclosure Interpretations. As recently as December 2022, the staff clarified that a non-GAAP measure excluding “normal, recurring, cash operating expenses necessary to operate a registrant’s business” could be misleading.

Question: Can certain adjustments, although not explicitly prohibited, result in a non-GAAP measure that is misleading?

Answer: Yes. Certain adjustments may violate Rule 100(b) of Regulation G because they cause the presentation of the non-GAAP measure to be misleading. Whether or not an adjustment results in a misleading non-GAAP measure depends on a company’s individual facts and circumstances.

Presenting a non-GAAP performance measure that excludes normal, recurring, cash operating expenses necessary to operate a registrant’s business is one example of a measure that could be misleading.

When evaluating what is a normal operating expense, the staff considers the nature and effect of the non-GAAP adjustment and how it relates to the company’s operations, revenue generating activities, business strategy, industry and regulatory environment.

The staff would view an operating expense that occurs repeatedly or occasionally, including at irregular intervals, as recurring (December 13, 2022).

Source: SEC.gov | Non-GAAP Financial Measures

Of course, interpreting the guidance is a matter of judgment that issuers make with their disclosure counsel, but our view from an investor perspective is that tariffs are normal, recurring, cash operating expenses necessary to operate a business, especially if that business already has an established history of paying tariffs or economically similar taxes in various countries.

A company may be able to argue that a change in tariff rates, especially a significant change, is not “normal” if such changes have happened rarely, which may be the case for the Harley-Davidson example. In those cases, it is useful for investors to be able to size the impact of the change, while still acknowledging that tariffs are a “real” cost that may not be temporary.

We think it should be left up to investors to exclude the impact of tariffs, or not

We think company managers “adjusting out” the impact of tariffs is inappropriate, akin to adjusting out rising or falling commodity prices. Tariffs are (in the short run, at least) unavoidable costs of doing business, recurring, and settled in cash. To the extent they are not fully passed through to customers — or if they cause demand destruction — tariffs are a loss of value of investors, as evidenced by the recent contraction in share prices.

That said, managers scoping and quantifying the impact of tariffs on their business, even if it’s an estimate, is helpful for investors to parse out the drivers of performance from one period to the next. If investors want to exclude the impact of tariffs from earnings or other measures, perhaps with expectation that the tariffs are temporary, that is their decision.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Trump's Tariffs Circle Pharma Industry: Who It Affects And How It May Play Out
data.description:
Investment Overview

The Trump administration's imposition of tariffs on goods imported into the U.S. has sent shockwaves across the globe and created turmoil across most if not all, stock markets around the world.

The S&P 500 (SPX) is down nearly 10% across the past 5 days and down >15% year-to-date, its biggest crash since the COVID pandemic was declared in early 2020. President Trump is apparently in no mood to back down. However, despite rapid retaliation from countries globally to his own retaliatory or "reciprocal" tariffs.

The next target appears to be the Pharmaceuticals industry - during a speech made at the National Republican Congressional Committee dinner last night (Tuesday, 8th April), the President suggested that a "major tariff" would be announced "very shortly." The President was also quoted as saying:

We’re going to do something that we have to do. We’re going to put tariffs on our pharmaceuticals and once we do that, they’re going to come rushing back into our country because we’re the big market.

The advantage we have over everybody is that we’re the big market. So we’re going to be announcing very shortly a major tariff on pharmaceuticals.

The effect on share prices of "Big Pharma" companies globally has been immediate and significant, as we can see in the table below:

The table above only looks at U.S. and European Pharma companies - Japanese Pharmas' Takeda Pharmaceutical Company Limited (TAK) and Astellas Pharma shares are down >2% and >6% today, while Indian Pharmas Sun Pharmaceutical and Dr. Reddy's Laboratories Limited (RDY) stock is down 2% and 0.5%, and drugmaker Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Limited (TEVA) down 6%.

Additionally, Jazz Pharmaceuticals plc (JAZZ), Perrigo Company plc (PRGO), and Alkermes plc (ALKS), all U.S. Pharmas domiciled in Ireland, benefitting from a lower tax regime, manufacturing products in the country and exporting it to the US, are down 6%, 3% and 6% in trading so far today.

Returning to the table above, the average monthly decline in share price for these 17 U.S. and EU companies is -13% - in short, Pharma fears the worst - but does the current fear, uncertainty and doubt, or "FUD," as traders call it, and market turbulence potentially creating a contrarian buying opportunity?

Diabetes/Weight Loss Giants Eli Lilly & Novo Nordisk Suffer Despite Pledges To Manufacture In The U.S.

Pharma companies have been protected to an extent from tariffs because shortages of certain drugs can result in patient deaths, but the President wants companies to manufacture more and more of their product within the U.S., and is believed to be using tariffs to persuade them to do that.

The President may point to Eli Lilly and Company's (LLY) decision to commit to building four new manufacturing plants in the U.S., at a cost of ~$27bn, which was announced at a press conference referred to as "Lilly in America," as a sign that his plan is working. That is questionable, however.

Lilly is the world's largest Pharma by market cap - $655bn at the time of writing, but much closer to $800bn prior to the market sell-off. This is largely thanks to its marketing and selling its glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide ("GIP") and glucagon-like peptide-1 ("GLP-1") mimetic, tirzepatide, under the brand name Mounjaro to treat Type 2 diabetes, and Zepbound to treat obesity.

This drug is forecast to drive peak revenues well exceeding $50bn per annum, and perhaps substantially more, so the Trump administration may be celebrating its decision to invest so much in U.S. manufacturing. With that said, Eli Lilly's CEO David Ricks has told the BBC that:

We don't support tariffs to be clear. In pharma, about 70% of global R&D takes place in the United States. So, we're creating the next generation of breakthroughs and cures. But the production is heavily weighted outside the US. And that's not unique to our industry. It happened with electronics and software and other things.

Ricks added that "we can't breach" agreements around drug pricing in Europe and will "have to eat the cost of the tariffs and make trade-offs within our own companies," meaning reduced spending on R&D and laying off staff. Despite Lilly's commitment to the U.S., its share price is down nearly 20% in the past month.

The news is arguably even worse for Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), whose drug semaglutide, a GLP-1 agonist, is tirzepatide's biggest rival, marketed and sold as Wegovy in weight loss, and Ozempic in Type 2 diabetes, and earning revenues of nearly $25bn in 2024.

The company is Danish. However, its management has been quoted as saying it is not worried about tariffs, despite Trump's apparently telling reporters on Air Force One last week that they may be "at a level that you haven't really seen before," and allegedly suggesting they could go even higher than a widely expected figure of ~25%.

This is possibly because Novo has a large manufacturing presence in the US, making $16.5bn to acquire U.S. drugmaker Catalent last year. The company also pointed out that it does not manufacture generic drugs, which may be the hardest hit by new tariffs. Nevertheless, its shares are down >10% on a 5-day basis and down >50% on an annual basis.

Will Generic Drugmakers Be Worst Hit In Regions Like India, Or European Pharmas, Who Plan To Slash R&D Budgets?

The reality is that all the Big Pharma companies in the table above source products from all over the world, most notably perhaps China, which has had tariffs of 104% imposed by the Trump administration and has promised to retaliate with "resolute and forceful" measures of its own.

The BBC says that the U.S. imported $213bn worth of medicines in 2024, while the EU exports $127bn of pharmaceutical products to the US and India, ~$13bn. As mentioned, the likes of Teva, Sun Pharma, Dr Reddy's, Cipla, Gland Pharma, Aurobindo Pharmaceuticals, Lupin, Biocon, and Glenmark Pharmaceuticals would surely see their business hit hard by additional tariffs, on top of the 27% blanket tariff imposed on all imports from India, as per Trump's announcement in the White House Rose Garden last week.

The idea is that medicines made and active pharmaceutical ingredients ("APIs") supplied by these companies could be made in the U.S. instead, but generic drugs don't command the same high prices that patent-protected drugs, so it's unclear if this would be an attractive industry that could take hold in the US.

That may, in turn, lead to Americans paying even more for their drugs domestically. As I related in an earlier post today on UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH), U.S. citizens pay up to ten times more for "miracle" weight loss drugs like Wegovy today. Potentially, they may be paying similarly inflated prices for generic drugs, too.

Back in Europe, members of the industry trade lobby the European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations ("EFPIA"), which includes the likes of Bayer, Novartis AG (NVS), and Novo Nordisk, plus U.S. companies Pfizer Inc. (PFE) Lilly, Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), GSK plc (GSK), and Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), plus Teva, have urged European Commission ("EC") President Ursula von der Leyen to act, stating:

Unless Europe delivers rapid, radical policy change, then pharmaceutical research, development, and manufacturing is increasingly likely to be directed towards the US.

The association has suggested that "€16.5bn, i.e., 10% of the total investment plans is at risk" with companies indicating planned spending in the U.S. would be in the region of €164.8bn.

Meanwhile, in Ireland, sentiment had been positive that its Pharma industry, an invaluable source of revenues that has helped to fuel the "Celtic Tiger" economy, had avoided tariffs for the time being. The country apparently exported ~€58 of pharma products to the U.S. last year, out of a total of €72.6bn across all industries.

President Trump has formerly suggested that:

"Ireland was very smart. They took our pharmaceutical companies away from presidents that didn’t know what they were doing and it's too bad that happened."

There is, therefore, a risk that U.S. Pharmas located in Ireland and manufacturing there could remain in the country for tax reasons, but relocate manufacturing back to the U.S.

Is There Method To The Tariff Madness? Could Tariffs On Pharma Be A Positive Development Long-Term

This is a hard question to answer, in my view, as it's hard to be certain how committed the Trump administration is to introducing these proposed tariffs or what precise form they may take. President Trump is seen in some quarters as a businessman turned politician who is arguably more comfortable being a dealmaker than a traditional political maneuverer.

A traditional politician might be less vocal or do more of their work behind the scenes, out of the public eye, but President Trump has a well-known predilection for deal-making - his book is titled "The Art of the Deal," after all. In the world of deal-making, the first offer is rarely the same as the last offer - negotiations take place, and bluff and bluster can be deployed similarly to playing a game of poker.

As such, in my view, it is possible that Trump may be using the threat of tariffs to achieve other goals, some of which may be hidden from sight. But what would be the point of this? President Trump does seem to sincerely want Pharma companies to begin manufacturing as much product as they possibly can in the U.S., but I'd speculate the likelihood of, e.g., Dr. Reddy's or Teva relocating manufacturing to the U.S. seems remote.

The Trump administration will likely be well aware that when it comes to voter's priorities when choosing whom to elect, "it's the economy, stupid." With no election until 2028, we might speculate the Trump administration believes this is the right time to play "fast and loose" with the economy and the tariff policy to test the water. If the consequences of tariffs begin to look disastrous, the administration could take them back off the table knowing that in three years' time, they may have been largely replaced in voters' thoughts by more recent considerations.

The administration may want to see "who blinks first" but would likely not risk a policy that will virtually guarantee they lose the next election by creating, for example, a recession or worse. President Trump may even be acting in the belief that Big Pharma is a deeply unpopular industry according to voters and believes that scaremongering CEOs and countries who may make much less for their drugs would be a popular policy.

Ultimately, all of this is speculation, and to date, all we know is that tariffs on Pharma companies are not in place, but may be planned. The logistics of manufacturing a drug entirely in one country are extremely complex - today, drugs are assembled in multiple different countries on their way to wherever their final destination may be.

Pharma tariffs are, therefore, a complex and fluid discussion - is what President Trump is suggesting even remotely achievable - could drug shortages across the U.S., and perhaps across the world, be a possibility if tariffs are introduced?

I would not necessarily rule it out, and I would question if there is any motivation at any Pharma company to manufacture entirely in the U.S., and yet, at the same time, it's possible to conceive of certain drugs being manufactured in one country.

Looking ahead, then, and considering whether the current turmoil could be represented as a contrarian trade opportunity, I see a few different options.

If President Trump elects for the "shock and awe" policy of actually introducing tariffs today and asking questions later, I'd predict that Big Pharma valuations will continue to tumble with little prospect of their being back at pre-2024 elections within five years.

If I am accurate in my assumption that President Trump's latest comments are the first gambit in what promises to be a lengthy series of negotiations, it's possible that stocks will become increasingly volatile, which could be good news for a day trader, but I still don't see valuations trending upwards much, if at all, from where they are today.

The task of relocating drug manufacturing back to the U.S., in my view, is a 15-20 year process, perhaps even a >50-year one. If President Trump goes ahead and tries to introduce a Mark Zuckerberg "move fast and break things" approach to conducting the pharmaceutical orchestra, the chaos that will ensue may rubber stamp an election victory for the Democrat candidate in 2028, but it won't do much for Pharma valuations, and if you have high exposure to Pharma in your portfolio, my conclusion would be there is cause for concern.

The even bigger problem is, if you do decide to sell today, what other industry can you invest in when thanks to "tariff-gate" the ebbing economic tide appears to be dragging pretty much every ship underwater.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vulcan Materials: Crushing It With Strong Pricing Power Even Amidst A Softer Macro Backdrop
data.description:
Introduction

Vulcan Materials Company (NYSE:VMC) is a construction aggregates supplier that holds #1 and #2 market positions in 90% of the markets it serves, operating a quasi-monopoly business that holds significant pricing power. Through this pricing power, Vulcan Materials can often increase prices exceeding the inflation rate and above that of its cost inflation, leading to higher ROICs and free cash flow margins. In the company's latest results, despite a small volume decline, Vulcan Materials' pricing power was on display with 11% price growth against 5% cost inflation. While the stock has sold off about 15% since my initial 'buy' recommendation in December, roughly in line with the 16% decline in the S&P 500 over the same time period, I think Vulcan Materials' is a name you want to own in this environment even amidst potentially softer private investment in the near-term.

Vulcan Materials Q4 Results

Vulcan Materials closed out 2024 with a solid quarter that beat on both the top and bottom line. During the company’s most recent results, Vulcan put up revenues of $1.85 billion, which surpassed sell-side estimates by $44.5 million. On EBITDA, the company’s $550 million in adjusted EBITDA represented a 16% increase compared to last year and a 29.7% margin with 370bps of margin expansion. As a result of the margin expansion, the company’s $2.17 in EPS was a 48.6% increase from last year with EPS surpassing sell-side estimates by 42 cents.

When we look at what drove the company’s performance in the latest quarter, Vulcan Materials Aggregates segment led the way as cash gross profit per ton surged 16% to $11.50 fueled by an 11% rise in freight-adjusted pricing against a tempered 5% uptick in unit cash costs. To me, this reflects strong pricing power and cost control with FY’24 adjusted EBITDA hitting $2.1 billion and margins expanding 190 basis points, as shown below.

We can also see that compared to prior years, the company’s aggregates volume in tons hasn’t really changed. In fact, it’s actually declined about 3% compared to last year. But, as is true for both Asphalt Mix and Ready-mixed concrete, the increasing average sales price more than offsets minor volume declines.

Ultimately, it’s these drivers that tell me the business is pretty solid and performing well. In terms of the company’s ROIC, a measure of profitability for every dollar of capital employed, Vulcan Materials’ 16.2% ROIC hasn’t fluctuated much and has remained pretty steady, as illustrated below. And even though aggregate shipments fell 3% year over year, there were still some bright spots in volume as public construction growth and storm recovery demand in regions like Western North Carolina offset weaker private activity. To me, this demonstrates that Vulcan’s pricing power carries more weight than volume growth. With an 11% price increase for the full year compared to unit cash costs rising just 5%, there are clearly efficiency gains and evidence of pricing power. Thus, a company like Vulcan Materials should do very well in periods of higher inflation, which I’ll touch on more when discussing potential tariff impacts.

Given the free cash flow generation of Vulcan Materials’ business, the company can afford to return substantial cash back to shareholders without having to invest much into capex. During the full year, the company deployed $2.3 billion toward acquisitions and $638 million into operations and greenfield projects which I believe sets the stage for sustained value creation. Meanwhile, $313 million went back to shareholders via dividends and stock repurchases. I think the pace of this can continue because of the balance sheet strength.

In terms of the company’s financial position, leverage remains low with Net Debt to EBITDA of 2.3x even after issuing $2 billion in notes (but planning to redeem $400 million in 2025 notes with cash). The leverage feels prudent given the company’s investment grade ratings, laddered maturities, and mostly fixed-rate debt (89.8%).

The current macro backdrop isn’t all that bad

Management seemed to have confidence in Vulcan Materials’ near-term outlook, as evidenced by the guidance provided on the company’s conference call following the quarter. In terms of the outlook, management laid out their forecast for adjusted EBITDA expected to land between $2.35 billion and $2.55 billion which signals to me another year of meaningful growth off their $2.1 billion 2024 base. Aggregates pricing is anticipated to rise 5% to 7% though that includes a 100bps drag from recent acquisitions, something I think they’ll overcome quickly given their track record. Shipments are pegged to grow 3% to 5% driven by those acquisitions and steady public construction demand which I feel offsets the softer private side. Unit cash costs are expected to climb in the low to mid-single digits and I believe this moderation paired with pricing gains should keep margins expanding. To me, this paints a picture of a business poised to deliver double-digit unit profitability growth in the short term which is no small feat with private construction still lagging.

Looking longer term I’m optimistic about where Vulcan is headed. The emphasis on public infrastructure can be seen through $122 billion in trailing 12-month highway starts and $45 billion in new state funding. As funding from the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act starts flowing through and residential and private non-residential activity starts to pick up, it feels like a matter of ‘when, not if’ with single-family starts ticking up and data center activity buzzing near Vulcan’s footprint. Therefore, the next few years could see a real inflection point once interest rates ease and private demand kicks back in. I don’t see them resting on their laurels either; they’re clearly playing offense with capital deployment and operational tweaks and low leverage could support future M&A.

Risks to consider

That said, I think investors need to balance this outlook, keeping in mind the macro backdrop and recession fears. When asked about the impact that the company has seen or expects to see regarding tariffs, CEO Tom Hill noted the following:

So I don't think we see any impact from policy on the public demand. It's IIJA, what you're seeing is the growth in public going to work. And remember, that money is protected through dedicated long-term funding. So nothing is going to happen to it. Looking forward, we would think this government will support traditional aggregate-intensive public work legislature. So probably a positive from that perspective. On tariffs, -- on aggregate tariffs directly, we see very little impact on everything else, and we've looked at steel and rubber. I'm not sure anyone can tell you what's going to happen, but I don't think it's a big impact to us. And on the flip side of that is I'm confident that Vulcan Materials teams will navigate whatever comes at us.

Look, we've seen a pandemic. We've seen volumes down. We've seen record inflation and our teams consistently grow unit margins and earnings, and that's exactly why we develop Vulcan selling and Vulcan operating so that we can consistently grow our unit profitability regardless of any outside challenges. So the government, I think, supports infrastructure, and I don't think we'll handle whatever comes at us on the tariffs.

Given the fact that a fair bit of demand is coming from the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, I actually think Vulcan Materials could come out of this as a winner. While there could be some short-term weakness given cold snaps having already hit in January and February, Vulcan Materials may actually benefit from a more inflationary environment (in a scenario where this is brought on by tariffs), meaning that the company’s pricing power becomes more pronounced and that margins expansion can accelerate.

Obviously, there is some element of political risk here (if the IIJA were to be ripped up or kiboshed in some form) and a recession would no doubt hurt the private construction demand (be that residential or non-residential) of Vulcan Materials’ business. But while shipments could stagnate beyond 2025’s 3% to 5% growth guide, a ‘stagflation’ environment might actually be an okay outcome for Vulcan Materials.

Valuation is justified

Analysts are bullish on Vulcan Materials’ outlook over the next year. With 24 sell-side analysts covering the stock, there are currently 16 ‘buy’ ratings, seven ‘hold’ ratings, and one ‘sell’ rating. With an average price target of $293.79, analysts see about 30.4% upside over the next twelve months, excluding the company’s dividend yield of 0.9%.

As for the valuation, Vulcan isn’t a cheap stock relative to other building materials companies. With the peer group average at 16.7x P/E and 9.9x, Vulcan looks optically expensive at 25.9x P/E and 13.8x EV/EBITDA (source: Bloomberg). However, in my view, I think there’s a case to be made as to why the company deserves to trade at a higher multiple than the group. For one thing, Vulcan Materials has tremendous pricing power that you simply wouldn’t get with those more exposed to wood products. With EPS having grown at a 9.1% CAGR over the last decade, I think it’s worth paying a premium to the peer group at a multiple closer to a market multiple (source: S&P Capital IQ).

In conclusion, Vulcan Materials has demonstrated strong performance in Q4 through pricing power which has been highlighted by strong revenue growth and margin expansion which ultimately has led to bottom line EPS growth. Despite a slight decline in aggregate volumes in Q4, the company’s pricing power and cost control have helped mitigate these challenges. Looking ahead, the favorable public infrastructure environment and flexible balance sheet positions the company well for continued success. While macroeconomic risks through a potential recession driven by the impact of tariffs could be on the horizon, I think Vulcan Materials is a name to own in this environment. While Vulcan trades at a premium valuation compared to its peers, the company’s track record, market position, and relatively low leverage make a compelling case for its higher multiple. For these reasons, I'm maintaining my 'buy' rating.

data.title: NML: A Solid Energy Infrastructure CEF But Not The Most Tax Efficient Structure
data.description:
(Data below is sourced from the Neuberger Berman website, unless otherwise stated.)

Overview

The Neuberger Berger Energy Infrastructure and Income Fund (NYSE:NML) uses a sector-based investment strategy and primarily invests in midstream infrastructure investments along with other energy companies, including renewables.

  • They focus on companies that provide essential services to move stores and process energy.

  • Their objectives are to provide an attractive total return along with high cash distributions.

  • They look for companies with growth potential, strong balance sheets and coverage ratios, scale, geographic and business-line diversity, and high-quality counterparty exposure along with sustainable cash flow.

  • The fund uses leverage via a secured margin facility.

The company uses one 1099 and no K-1s, which simplifies tax reporting.

NML has an inception date of March 2013 and currently trades at an 11% discount to NAV. This is in line with other CEF peers in the MLP sector, such as (TYG), (EMO) and (KYN). (SRV) is an exception and trades at about a 10% premium, probably because of its very high distribution yield.

NML Does Not Issue a K-1 - But Corporate Taxes Must be Paid

NML is one of the few 1940 Act funds that must pay corporate income tax. Under the 1940 Act, mutual funds and ETFs that are corporations can still qualify to be an RIC or Regulated Investment Company. These are exempt from corporate tax so long as they meet certain rules.

Some examples of these rules are:

  • Must be fully invested in securities

  • Must meet certain diversification thresholds.

  • Must Invest less than 25% of assets in Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs)

NML fails the last rule and currently has about 35% of the portfolio invested in MLPs. Because of this, they are subject to corporate income tax.

The fund does discuss the tax liability in the fund fact sheet, but the discussion is somewhat buried in a long footnote:

"Because of its concentration in MLP investments, the Fund is not eligible to be a regulated investment company under the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended (the "Code"). Accordingly, the Fund will be treated as a taxable regular corporation, or so called "C" corporation, for federal tax purposes ("'C' corporation").

As a result, the Fund will be subject to federal income tax on its taxable income at the graduated rates applicable to corporations (currently at a maximum rate of 21%) as well as state and local income taxes. As a consequence of this concentration, the aggregate returns the Fund realizes may be adversely affected if a small number of investments perform poorly. Given that the Fund will be treated as a "C" corporation for federal tax purposes, it will incur tax expenses. In calculating its NAV in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles, the Fund will, among other things, account for its deferred tax liability and/or asset balances. The Fund will accrue a deferred income tax liability balance, at the currently effective maximum statutory federal income tax rate (currently 21%) plus an estimated state and local income tax rate, for its future tax liability associated with the capital appreciation of its investments and the distributions it receives on equity securities of MLPs considered to be returns of capital and for any net operating gains.

Any deferred tax liability balance will reduce the Fund's NAV. On the Fund's sale of a portfolio security, it may recognize gains for federal, state and local income tax purposes, which may result in income taxes imposed on the Fund. No assurance can be given that such taxes will not exceed the Fund's deferred tax liability assumptions for purposes of computing its NAV per share, which would result in an immediate reduction of that value, which could be material."

Here are the top 10 fund holdings as of December 31, 2024.

Portfolio Allocation Strategy

The fund had about 35% invested in master limited partnerships as of year-end. Most of the investments are in large cap and mid-cap stocks, although a small slice of around 10% of the portfolio is invested in stocks with a market cap below $10 billion.

Within the energy sector, the fund is most heavily invested in the natural gas segment, with 71.3% of the portfolio. The portfolio is mainly invested in midstream companies at 66.9%.

NML - Past Performance

Over the longer term, NML has produced decent performance, and it has received a 4-star Morningstar rating.

NAV

Source: Morningstar

Consistent Distribution History

In December 2022, NML raised the monthly distribution per share from $0.0227 to $0.0584. Since January 2024, all distributions have been ROC or return of capital. Because of this, the fund has been a good holding for either taxable or IRA accounts.
